title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman argues that the pro-life movement's gradualist, court-deferring strategy is morally bankrupt and self-defeating, and that states must instead defy Roe v. Wade through immediatist nullification bills modeled on Elizabeth Heyrick's 1824 case against gradual emancipation. He treats Oklahoma SB13 and the Wisconsin nullification of the Fugitive Slave Act as the proper template, calling Christians to demand the immediate, total abolition of abortion as murder.
audiences: Pro-life Christian considering whether incrementalism actually works
State legislator weighing a nullification or equal-protection bill
Constitutional conservative skeptical of defying the Supreme Court
Abolitionist activist looking for historical and theological grounding
Pastor or church leader discipling congregants on abortion

questions.question: What does Silberman say is wrong with treating reductions in abortion under Roe as 'winning'?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering whether incrementalism actually works
questions.answer: He concedes that incremental reductions are the most the Supreme Court permits, but insists this is winning a rigged game whose legitimacy abolitionists should reject outright. The proper response is to ignore Roe and demand outright abolition rather than treat 822,000 murders a year as a victory.
questions.quote: Because of our strict adherence to *Roe* v. Wade, we’re left settling for the fact that *only* 822,000 or so babies were murdered in 2015 and considering that a win. In a way, that is winning insofar as it’s the closest to winning the Supreme Court permits us to come. However, it’s winning a fraudulent game which we shouldn’t be treating as legitimate. We need to ignore *Roe* and abolish abortion.

questions.question: How does the article characterize the constitutional legitimacy of Roe v. Wade?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative skeptical of defying the Supreme Court
questions.answer: Silberman cites left-leaning legal scholars to argue Roe has no defensible constitutional basis and amounts to a naked power grab by the Court. On this view, Roe is not law to be respected but tyranny to be defied.
questions.quote: Roe’s most damning indictment comes from former dean of Stanford Law school John Hart Ely who [proclaimed that](https://www.washingtonexaminer.com/in-criticizing-roe-sessions-aligns-with-most-legal-scholars) “[*Roe v. Wade*] is not constitutional law and gives almost no sense of an obligation to try to be.” What Ely is saying is that in *Roe*, Blackmun hardly tried to make his decision appear legitimate. *Roe*amounts to nothing more than simple tyranny; a transparent power grab by a branch of government determined to become the engine of societal upheaval.

questions.question: What does the article propose state legislatures actually do about Roe?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing a nullification or equal-protection bill
questions.answer: It points to Oklahoma SB13 as the model: legislatively recognize unborn children as persons entitled to equal protection from fertilization, and treat any contrary federal statute, regulation, executive order, or court ruling as void. This is framed as the appropriate, even the only, lawful response when a federal entity declares a class of humans non-persons.
questions.quote: “It is the intent of the Legislature,” the bill states, “to provide to unborn children the equal protection of the laws of this state; to establish that a living human child, from the moment of fertilization upon the fusion of a human spermatozoon with a human ovum, is entitled to the same rights, powers, privileges, justice and protections as are secured or granted by the laws of this state to any other human person; and to treat as void and of no effect any and all federal statutes, regulations, executive orders and court rulings, which would deprive an unborn child of the right to life.”

questions.question: What historical precedent does Silberman invoke for nullifying Roe?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist looking for historical and theological grounding
questions.answer: He grounds the strategy in Wisconsin's 1854 nullification of the Fugitive Slave Act, presenting nullification as the rightful response when the federal government strips a class of humans of personhood. He bluntly rejects 'just following orders' as a defense for any modern complicity.
questions.quote: Following in the footsteps of Wisconsin’s 1854 nullification of the Fugitive Slave Act, OK SB13 is the appropriate response to a federal entity declaring a group of humans non-persons. Dare I say defiance and nullification is the *only*appropriate response to such a ruling. “I’m just following orders” wasn’t a legitimate excuse for mass murder-enablers of the past and it isn’t a legitimate excuse now.

questions.question: Why does the article cite Elizabeth Heyrick's 1824 pamphlet at such length?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist looking for historical and theological grounding
questions.answer: Heyrick argued that the call for gradual emancipation originated with the slaveholder because gradualism breeds indifference and moral acclimation, and Silberman draws the parallel directly to abortion. The pro-life movement, on his read, has been seduced by the same satanic sophistry that defanged the early antislavery cause.
questions.quote: “The slave holder knew very well that his prey would be secure, so long as the abolitionists could be cajoled into a demand for gradual instead of immediate abolition. He knew very well, that the contemplation of a gradual emancipation, would beget a gradual indifference to emancipation itself. He knew very well, that even the wise and the good, may, by habit and familiarity, be brought to endure and tolerate almost any thing…

questions.question: What does Silberman say has happened to the church under the incremental strategy?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader discipling congregants on abortion
questions.answer: He says millions of Christians have been gradually anesthetized to abortion by being told to settle for ultrasound laws, waiting periods, and small percentage reductions. Settling for those wins, he argues, lulls the church to sleep and prevents the only demand that could actually end abortion.
questions.quote: A faithful, uncompromising delineation of the horrors of abortion by the hundreds of millions of Christians in this country would bring abortion to an end. But if those millions of Christians settle for Right to Life, and Right to Life-allied centrist Republicans passing ultrasound laws and waiting periods which reduce abortion by one or two or five percent and call that winning, there will never be an end to abortion because the church will have been lulled to sleep and the actual demand that abortion be outlawed because it is murder will never have been made.

questions.question: How does the article answer worries that nullifying Roe would create a 'crisis of authority'?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative skeptical of defying the Supreme Court
questions.answer: Silberman invokes Jefferson's 1820 letter to William Jarvis to argue that treating the Supreme Court as the final arbiter of all constitutional questions establishes an oligarchy the Constitution never authorized. The departments are co-equal, and judges are not immune from the same passions as other officials.
questions.quote: “You seem to consider the judges as the ultimate arbiters of all constitutional questions; a very dangerous doctrine indeed, and one which would place us under the despotism of an oligarchy. Our judges are as honest as other men, and not more so. They have, with others, the same passions for party, for power, and the privilege of their corps…. Their power [is] the more dangerous as they are in office for life, and not responsible, as the other functionaries are, to the elective control. The Constitution has erected no such single tribunal, knowing that to whatever hands confided, with the corruptions of time and party, its members would become despots. It has more wisely made all the departments co-equal and co-sovereign within themselves.”

questions.question: Does the article think immediatism makes Roe's reversal more or less likely?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering whether incrementalism actually works
questions.answer: Silberman argues the immediatist position is actually more likely to bring Roe down, because the Court's authority depends on the deference it commands. A state openly defying judicial activism signals that the people will no longer tolerate it and gives the Court reason to retreat.
questions.quote: The immediatist position is that the Supreme Court’s permission is not necessary to abolish abortion, but the immediatist position is also more likely to bring about Roe’s reversal. A state challenging the court’s self-appointed authority to legalize murder signals to judges and justices that the people will no longer put up with judicial activism and legislation from the bench. The Supreme Court has no enforcement power meaning their authority is contingent upon the degree to which it respects the constitution and the respect it commands from the other branches, from the states, and from the people.

questions.question: How does Blackmun's footnote 54 in Roe figure into the argument against compromised pro-life laws?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing a nullification or equal-protection bill
questions.answer: Blackmun observed that the Texas statute treated abortion inconsistently with murder, exempting the woman and imposing only a short prison sentence on the abortionist, and used that inconsistency to undermine personhood. Silberman argues this proves that pro-life laws which exempt mothers and treat abortion as something less than murder actively sabotage the case against it.
questions.quote: “There are other inconsistencies between Fourteenth Amendment status and the typical abortion statute. It has already been pointed out, n. 49, supra, that in Texas the woman is not a principal or an accomplice with respect to an abortion upon her. If the fetus is a person, why is the woman not a principal or an accomplice? Further, the penalty for criminal abortion specified by Art. 1195 is significantly less than the maximum penalty for murder prescribed by Art. 1257 of the Texas Penal Code. If the fetus is a person, may the penalties be different?”

questions.question: What parallel does Silberman draw between Roe v. Wade and Dred Scott?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist looking for historical and theological grounding
questions.answer: He treats both rulings as evil and unconstitutional acts that legally severed humanity from personhood, the pinnacle of injustice. When courts go that far, he argues, the states are obligated to disregard them rather than wait for the Court's permission to do right.
questions.quote: Whether it be *Roe v. Wade* or *Scott v. Sanford*, the legal separation of humanity from personhood is the pinnacle of injustice. Both were evil and unconstitutional rulings, and when the courts disregard the constitution so egregiously and deliberately as to legalize murder, the states must disregard the courts.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Abolitionists Rising's pillar statement of the same immediatist principle this article defends.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Companion explainer for the state-level nullification strategy Silberman points to in OK SB13.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Develops the equal-protection-from-fertilization standard the SB13 quote in this piece embodies.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the 'Villains' of American History?
related.reason: Direct follow-up defending nullification against the historical objections raised by Roe's defenders.

related.title: Don't Fall For Texas Right to Life's Faux Abolitionism
related.reason: Applies the immediatist critique to a specific pro-life organization repackaging incrementalism as abolition.

related.title: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
related.reason: Concrete case of the Republican and pro-life-lobby opposition to abolition bills that this article warns about.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Sharpens the pro-life vs. abolitionist distinction that frames Silberman's whole argument.
